notes: Arturia Synthi V emulates the EMS Synthi AKS, the iconic portable synthesizer used by Pink Floyd, Brian Eno, Klaus Schulze, and countless experimental composers in the 1970s. Unlike conventional synthesizers, the Synthi uses a pin matrix for modulation routing — small pins inserted into a grid connect any output to any input, creating chaotic, unpredictable, and infinitely variable signal flows. Three oscillators, a multi-mode filter, a trapezoid envelope, a spring reverb, and a joystick controller complete the architecture. The Synthi excels at drones, evolving textures, abstract sound design, and experimental music where the system's inherent instability is a feature rather than a flaw.

Modulation.description: Modulation wheel — assignable via pin matrix routing

Filter Frequency.description: Filter cutoff frequency — the Synthi filter is a multi-mode design with a distinctive character

Filter Resonance.description: Filter Q/resonance — creates the filter's characteristic peak

Osc1 Pitch.description: Oscillator 1 pitch — from sub-audio to above hearing range; cross-modulation creates FM-like timbres

Osc Sync.description: Oscillator sync/cross-modulation amount — the Synthi's inter-oscillator modulation creates unstable, evolving tones

LFO Rate.description: LFO rate — oscillator 3 rate when used as a low-frequency modulation source

Reverb.description: Built-in spring reverb level — integral to the Synthi's atmospheric sound

Joystick X.description: Joystick X axis — assignable controller for real-time performance gestures

Joystick Y.description: Joystick Y axis — second dimension of the performance joystick
